title: Are U.S.-India Ties Really Thawing?
gist: A US-India trade deal is reportedly 'in the last 1 percent' but mutual trust has waned since the 2025 India-Pakistan war and Trump's tariffs, H-1B fees, and Russian-oil sanctions; the author argues India's own rigidity on market access and 'strategic autonomy' worsened the damage.

insights.claim: US Ambassador Sergio Gor said the US-India trade deal is 'in the final steps... the last 1 percent,' after a tentative February agreement was upended when the Supreme Court invalidated Trump's sweeping tariffs.
insights.speaker: Sumit Ganguly
insights.so_what: A near-term deal catalyst for India-exposed trade and equities—watch for signing and tariff terms.

insights.claim: Two sticking points remain: India wants tariffs lower than rivals like Bangladesh, Malaysia, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, and Vietnam, while the US wants guarantees India isn't using forced labor for export advantage (Section 301).
insights.speaker: Sumit Ganguly
insights.so_what: Relative tariff levels vs. Asian competitors will determine India's export competitiveness post-deal.

insights.claim: US-India ties unraveled after the May 2025 India-Pakistan conflict, when Trump claimed he brokered the cease-fire via trade threats—India rejected the claim while Pakistan embraced it and called for Trump to receive a Nobel Prize.
insights.speaker: Sumit Ganguly
insights.so_what: Trump's mediation claim is a persistent irritant driving India-US friction—a political-risk overhang.

insights.claim: Trump imposed draconian tariffs on India, hefty H-1B visa fees (India the greatest beneficiary), and sanctions for Indian purchases of Russian oil, leaving New Delhi with few options but to endure.
insights.speaker: Sumit Ganguly
insights.so_what: Multiple concurrent US pressure points on India—tariffs, visas, oil sanctions—weigh on Indian IT and exporters.

insights.claim: Since Trump's May visit to China and meeting with Xi, Indian policymakers doubt New Delhi's value to Washington as a counterweight to Beijing.
insights.speaker: Sumit Ganguly
insights.so_what: A potential US-China thaw would erode India's strategic premium—reshaping its geopolitical positioning.

insights.claim: The author argues India's decades of tariff and nontariff barriers, unpredictable regulation, and IP/ownership restrictions worsened the US approach, and more flexibility could have blunted Trump's pressure.
insights.speaker: Sumit Ganguly
insights.so_what: Assigns agency to India's protectionism—relevant for assessing how reformable India's investment climate is.

insights.claim: India's 'strategic autonomy' is a nebulous replacement for nonalignment and, combined with continued dependence on Russia for arms, has hindered deeper US ties.
insights.speaker: Sumit Ganguly
insights.so_what: India's Russia-arms dependence and hedging cap how close the US-India partnership can become.

insights.claim: China rode out US tariff threats more easily than India because it enjoyed considerable economic leverage, having built trade advantages and attracted vast US investment.
insights.speaker: Sumit Ganguly
insights.so_what: Contrasts China's leverage with India's vulnerability—India lacks the reciprocal exposure to deter US pressure.

insights.claim: Despite tensions, India remains the US's 10th-largest trading partner with deepening naval cooperation (Exercise Malabar) and a growing technology partnership.
insights.speaker: Sumit Ganguly
insights.so_what: Structural ballast in the relationship supports a recovery case if the trade deal closes.
